coredata.dc:description: Extensive research has explored strategies to enhance permanganate (PM) oxidation capacity, with carbon materials having shown significant promise in treating refractory organic pollutants in water. However, the impacts of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and heteroatom-doped CQDs on PM oxidation remain unclear, warranting further investigation. In this study, we found that sulfur-doped CQDs (SCQDs) significantly outperformed CQDs, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs), and boron-doped CQDs (BCQDs) in promoting the oxidation reaction of diclofenac sodium (DCF) by PM. Through quenching experiments, light-shielding tests, and UV-Vis spectroscopy, we revealed that the PM/SCQDs system could rapidly and stably generate Mn(III) and MnO2, thereby facilitating the removal of DCF. Characterization analysis showed that SCQDs possess a surface enriched with diverse functional groups, and sulfur doping further enhances their electron cloud density. This structural characteristic enhances the ability of SCQDs to donate electrons to permanganate, thereby promoting its reduction to Mn(III) and MnO2. A comprehensive evaluation of key operational parameters governing the PM/SCQDs system performance was conducted. Additionally, the PM/SCQDs system exhibited substantial resistance to interference from common matrix ions and natural organic matter in water. The study provides a new perspective on enhancing permanganate oxidation by SCQDs and offers a promising pathway for efficient water pollutant degradation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Extensive research has explored strategies to enhance permanganate (PM) oxidation capacity, with carbon materials having shown significant promise in treating refractory organic pollutants in water. However, the impacts of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and heteroatom-doped CQDs on PM oxidation remain unclear, warranting further investigation. In this study, we found that sulfur-doped CQDs (SCQDs) significantly outperformed CQDs, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs), and boron-doped CQDs (BCQDs) in promoting the oxidation reaction of diclofenac sodium (DCF) by PM. Through quenching experiments, light-shielding tests, and UV-Vis spectroscopy, we revealed that the PM/SCQDs system could rapidly and stably generate Mn(III) and MnO2, thereby facilitating the removal of DCF. Characterization analysis showed that SCQDs possess a surface enriched with diverse functional groups, and sulfur doping further enhances their electron cloud density. This structural characteristic enhances the ability of SCQDs to donate electrons to permanganate, thereby promoting its reduction to Mn(III) and MnO2. A comprehensive evaluation of key operational parameters governing the PM/SCQDs system performance was conducted. Additionally, the PM/SCQDs system exhibited substantial resistance to interference from common matrix ions and natural organic matter in water. The study provides a new perspective on enhancing permanganate oxidation by SCQDs and offers a promising pathway for efficient water pollutant degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the mass production and widespread application of chemicals and pharmaceuticals have led to the emergence of numerous emerging organic contaminants (EOCs) in aquatic environments. Characterized by their persistence and potential toxicity, these pollutants can exert adverse effects on human health and environmental safety even at trace concentrations. Unfortunately, conventional water treatment technologies often prove inadequate in effectively mitigating these contaminants. Given the profound impacts of EOCs on ecosystems and public health, the issues associated with these compounds has garnered substantial extensive attention from researchers[1,2]. Since advanced oxidation process (AOP) can efficiently generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), which have strong oxidizing ability and can deeply oxidize organic pollutants and decompose them into harmless small molecules, thus effectively removing hard-to-degrade organic pollutants or pre-treating biologically intractable compounds into biodegradable substances, thus improving the efficiency of subsequent biological treatment. Therefore, AOP is widely recognized as a highly promising and efficient technological tool for the treatment of these EOCs. Compared with common treatment methods such as the Fenton reaction, photocatalytic oxidation[3], ozone oxidation, electrochemical oxidation[4], Low-valent sulfur oxy-acid salts[5], and persulfate oxidation, permanganate oxidation technology demonstrates significant competitiveness due to its unique chemical properties and operational advantages. Permanganate has the advantages of high chemical stability, low operating costs, and easy transport and storage. Additionally, it exhibits strong resistance to interference from complex water matrices during water treatment and their reduction product can further enhance impurity removal through coagulation These characteristics make permanganate oxidation a promising technology for water treatment[6]. However, PM has a mild oxidation potential (E0=1.68 V) and tends to react with electron-rich pollutants[7]. Therefore, recent research has been dedicated to enhancing the oxidation performance of permanganates through various strategies, including photoactivation[8], electroactivation[9], redox-mediated activation[10], and activation via carbon materials[11].

Carbon materials, including biochar, graphite, oxidized graphene, and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), are employed to facilitate the oxidation of PM owing to their superior characteristics such as high specific surface area, abundant surface functional groups, and eco-friendliness[12–15]. The enhancement mechanisms of PM oxidation by various carbon materials exhibit considerable differences, which can be categorized into two primary pathways: (1) the surface functional groups or structural defects of carbon materials facilitate the generation of reactive manganese intermediates (e.g., Mn(III)/Mn(IV)), thereby enhancing the oxidative capacity; and (2) carbon materials act as efficient electron mediators, enabling direct electron transfer from organic pollutants to permanganate[13]. Zhao et al. explored the activation of PM by CNTs and revealed that the introduction of CNTs markedly improved the oxidative degradation efficiency of PM for typical organic pollutants, including phenol and aniline[16]. By monitoring changes in permanganate and total manganese concentrations, they discovered that a substantial amount of MnO2 formed on the CNT surface following incorporation. Notably, at lower pH levels, CNTs more effectively degrade phenol, confirming that MnO2 generation enhances phenol oxidation. Tian et al. demonstrated that the addition of biochar significantly enhanced the degradation of SMX by permanganate[17]. They proposed that permanganate interacts with surface functional groups (e.g., C−H, CC, CO) on the biochar, leading to the formation of intermediate manganese species complexed with biochar. These biochar-bound intermediate manganese species exhibit stronger oxidative activity than permanganate alone, thus facilitating more efficient oxidation of SMX. Peng et al. found that graphite could mediate the transfer of electrons from SMX directly to PM, rather than induing RMnS generation via the interaction of PM with reductive sites on the carbon surface[11]. Meanwhile, Zhang et al. proposed that reduced graphene oxide (rGO) supports pollutant degradation through two concurrent pathways: First, rGO donates electrons to permanganate, generating highly reactive manganese species; second, rGO mediates the transfer of electrons from pollutants to permanganate[13].

CQDs, as a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particle sizes typically below 10 nm, exhibit unique optical, electrical and chemical properties. They have a high degree of dispersibility in water, abundant surface functional groups, and excellent electrical conductivity[18–20]. These unique characteristics have spurred interest in their application within AOPs. For instance, CQDs have been employed as visible-light catalysts for the activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and the remediation of aqueous organic dyes, leveraging their ability to inhibit photogenerated electron-hole recombination[21]. Wang et al. recently developed a CQDs/Co@CN dual-site catalyst showing excellent PMS activation. Interestingly, visible light could precisely regulate reactive species generation in this system. Experiments and calculations revealed CQDs' critical role in electron transfer and ROS modulation[22]. Additionally, CQDs have shown promise in Fenton-like systems. Zhang et al. demonstrated that the synergistic effect between defects and edge carboxylic groups in CQDs enhances Fenton-like catalysis, with CQDs facilitating the redox cycling of Fe(II/III) to accelerate Fe(II) regeneration[23]. Besides, many studies have endeavored to improve the functionality of CQDs by doping heterogeneous atoms to control or improve the optical, chemical, and electrical properties of CQDs[24]. Wang et al. investigated nitrogen-doped CQDs and found that N-doping not only increased the quantum yield but also modified their optical characteristics[25]. Owing to the comparable size of non-metallic elements with carbon atoms (C), it is feasible to modify the electronic structure of CQDs and induce structural defects. Recent studies indicated that the incorporating dopant elements such as nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), and boron (B) into CQDs can effectively modulate the framework and surface chemical structure of the quantum dots[26]. Wang et al. reported that N-doped CQDs (NCDs) coupled with g-C3N4 exhibited significantly higher pollutant degradation efficiency under visible light compared to g-C3N4 and undoped CQDs (CDs)/g-C3N4. This performance enhancement was attributed to the nitrogen doping that alters its fluorescence properties, extends the visible light absorption range, and enhances the electron transfer properties[27]. Although there are currently abundant studies on the enhancement of permanganate oxidation by different carbon materials, and CQDs have been applied in fields such as PMS oxidation and photocatalysis, the application of CQDs, especially heteroatom-doped CQDs, in the PM oxidation system remains unexploited[28]. In light of this, it is necessary to conduct relevant research to clarify their mechanisms and further optimize their applications in this field.

In this study, we evaluated the performance of CQDs and heteroatom-doped CQDs (e.g., NCQDs, SCQDs, and BCQDs) in enhancing permanganate oxidation to remove DCF. The effects of key operational parameters, such as pH, permanganate dosage, and SCQDs dosage on DCF degradation were systematically assessed. Additionally, the roles of various reactive species, including free radicals and reactive manganese species (RMnS) in the PM/SCQDs process were identified. The critical function of SCQDs within the PM/SCQDs system was elucidated and the underlying mechanisms by which this system enhances pollutant removal were clarified. The influence of common water matrix components on DCF degradation by the PM/SCQDs system was also investigated. Finally, the degradation pathway and toxicological changes of DCF were analyzed.

Bisphenol A (BPA), sulfisoxazole (SIZ), phenol (PE), diclofenac sodium (DCF), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), methyl phenyl sulfoxide (PMSO), methyl phenyl sulfone (PMSO2)， hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH2OH·HCl), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO), and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were supplied by Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol (MA), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), citric acid (CA), sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and permanganate (PM, KMnO4) were purchased from Chengdu KeLong Chemical Co., LTD. Sodium pyrophosphate (Na4P2O7) and glucose(C6H12O6) were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents used in the experiment were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. Deionized water was used as the solvent throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and heteroatom-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs and heteroatom-doped CQDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method based on previous studies[27,29–31]. To synthesize CQDs, 0.5 g of glucose was dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown solution was centrifuged and filtrated using a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The final solution was CQDs.

To synthesize SCQDs, 25 mL sodium citrate solution (0.1 M) and sodium thiosulfate (0.3 M) were transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 6 hours. Following this process, SCQDs with a carbon-to-sulfur ratio of 1:3 were obtained through filtration using a 0.22 μm membrane filter. By adjusting the concentration of sodium thiosulfate, it is feasible to synthesize SCQDs with carbon-to-sulfur ratios of either 1:1 or 1:5. As depicted in Fig. S1, SCQDs with varying carbon-to-sulfur ratios in enhancing the permanganate oxidation demonstrate distinct degradation efficiencies of DCF. Notably, the highest removal efficiency of DCF is achieved when the carbon-to-sulfur ratio is 1:3 (in the following text unless otherwise specified, SCQDs refer to those with a carbon-to-sulfur ratio of 1:3). The preparation methods of NCQDs and BCQDs are described in Text S1.

All experiments were performed in 150 mL beakers under magnetic stirring, with the reaction temperature maintained at 25 ± 1 °C using a water bath. The initial pH of the solution was adjusted by adding 0.01 M or 0.1 M H2SO4 and NaOH. Real-time pH monitoring revealed minimal fluctuations ( ± 0.2 units) during the reactions, demonstrating excellent system stability (Fig. S2). The batch reactions were initiated by introducing the PM solution into the mixture solutions containing the target organic pollutant and CQDs (or heteroatom-doped CQDs). At predetermined time intervals, 2 mL of the solution was collected and immediately quenched with an excess of hydroxylamine hydrochloride to terminate the reaction. The samples were then filtered through a 0.22 μm glass fiber membrane and analyzed for organic compound concentrations using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). All experiments were conducted in at least duplicate, and the average values along with error bars were presented.

The concentrations of residual pollutants were quantified using HPLC (Agilent 1260; 4.6 mm × 150 mm, 4 μm Agilent EC-C18 column). Detailed HPLC parameters for various organic compounds are provided in Table S1. The absorbance of the solution was measured with a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Methods for quantifying the concentrations of PM and MnO2 are described in Text S2. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to obtain high-definition images and detailed structural information of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-α) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Spectrum two FTIR) were used to analyze the functional groups present in the samples. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis was carried out to identify the generation of
O
2
• −
and 1O2 (BRUKER, ESR5000). The degraded intermediates of DCF were identified using high-performance liquid chromatography-quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometer (HPLC-QTOF-MS, Agilent G6545). Additional information on liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is provided in Text S3.

The synthesized SCQDs were characterized using TEM, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, and XPS. As depicted in Fig. 1(a), the TEM image revealed that the SCQDs had a uniform spherical morphology with a narrow size distribution ranging from 4.0 to 9.0 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra, shown in Fig. 1(b), further confirmed the successful synthesis of the SCQDs, demonstrating their distinct luminescent properties. Under excitation wavelengths of 320–340 nm, the SCQDs exhibited a strong emission peak centered at approximately 600 nm. These results collectively indicate that the synthesized SCQDs possess a particle size below 10 nm and exhibit pronounced fluorescence characteristics, thereby validating the successful preparation of quantum dots.

The surface functional groups of the SCQDs were characterized by FTIR and XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 1(c), the FTIR spectra revealed the presence of various surface functional groups on the SCQDs. A broad absorption band observed at 3373 cm⁻1 was attributed to O–H stretching vibrations, while the peak at 1558 cm⁻1 can be ascribed to the CS groups. The peak between 1396 and 1496 cm⁻¹ was identified as COO⁻ groups, and the peaks at 1096 cm⁻¹ and 995 cm⁻¹ were assigned to –SO3⁻ and C–O bonds, respectively. Additionally, the peaks at 675 cm⁻¹ and 613 cm⁻¹ were associated with C–S and C–S–C bending vibrations[30,32]. These findings indicate that the SCQDs possess a surface rich in diverse functional groups, including O–H, CS, COO⁻, –SO3⁻, and C–S. To further confirm the presence of these functional groups, XPS analysis was conducted on the SCQDs. The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s, S 2p and O 1 s spectra of SCQDs are presented in Fig. 1(d)-(f), with binding energies calibrated using the C 1 s peak at 284.5 eV. As shown in Fig. 1(d), the C 1 s spectrum can be resolved into the following components: C–C and C–H at 284.5 eV, C–S at 285.3 eV, and CO at 288.4 eV. The high-resolution scan of the S 2p spectrum, displayed in Fig. 1(e), exhibited three distinct energy states at 161.3 eV, 162.6 eV, and 168.5 eV, corresponding to the contributions of -C–SOx, -C–S, and -SO4, respectively. Furthermore, the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1(f)) revealed three peaks centered at 531.3 eV, 532.0 eV, and 535.8 eV, which were assigned to C–O, CO, and OC–OH, respectively[33,34]. The XPS results not only confirmed the functional groups identified by FTIR but also provided additional evidence for the successful incorporation of sulfur into the carbon quantum dots. Collectively, the FTIR and XPS analyses demonstrate that the SCQDs exhibit a diverse array of surface functional groups, with the presence of sulfur-containing groups further validating the effective synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permanganate oxidation performance enhanced by CQDs and heteroatom-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation efficiency of DCF was systematically compared among five different systems: PM alone, PM/CQDs, PM/NCQDs, PM/SCQDs, and PM/BCQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2(a). The addition of CQDs resulted in a marginal improvement in DCF degradation efficiency. In contrast, heteroatom-doped CQDs consistently exhibited a substantial enhancement in degradation performance, with SCQDs demonstrating the most remarkable effect. Specifically, the PM/SCQDs system achieved nearly complete DCF degradation (approaching 100 %) within 20 min, whereas the removal efficiencies of PM alone, PM/CQDs, PM/NCQDs, and PM/BCQDs were limited to 25 %, 45 %, 57 %, and 73 %, respectively. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 2(b), the first-order rate constants for DCF degradation were 0.014 min⁻1, 0.03 min⁻1, 0.181 min⁻1, 0.043 min⁻1, and 0.066 min⁻1 for the PM, PM/CQDs, PM/SCQDs, PM/NCQDs and PM/BCQDs systems, respectively. Notably, the PM/SCQDs system exhibited a first-order rate constant that was 13, 6, 4, and 3 times higher than those of the PM, PM/CQDs, PM/NCQDs, and PM/BCQDs systems, respectively. These findings conclusively establish that the incorporation of SCQDs substantially enhances the oxidative ability of permanganate, resulting in a remarkable increase in the degradation efficiency of DCF.

Furthermore, the PM/SCQDs system demonstrated significant potential for enhancing the degradation efficiency of various organic pollutants, including phenol, 2,4-DCP, BPA, and SIZ. In systems utilizing PM alone, the degradation rate of phenol was exceedingly low and nearly negligible. However, the removal rate of phenol increased to 56 % after the introduction of SCQDs. Concurrently, the removal rate of 2,4-DCP rose from 21 % to 53 %, the degradation of BPA improved from 56 % to a complete 100 % following the addition of SCQDs. Regarding SIZ, the degradation rate achieved by PM was restricted to 82 % within 20 min. In contrast, the PM/SCQDs system enabled the complete degradation of SIZ within merely 5 min (Fig S3). Moreover, the first-order rate constants for the degradation of phenol, 2,4-DCP, BPA, and SIZ in the PM/SCQDs system were determined to be 0.038 min⁻¹ , 0.137 min⁻¹ , 0.037 min⁻¹ , and 0.722 min⁻¹ , respectively. These values represent substantial improvements compared to the PM system alone, with enhancement factors of 13-fold, 3-fold, 4-fold, and 8-fold, respectively (Fig. 2(c), Fig. S4). As previously stated, the SCQDs significantly improve the oxidative capacity of PM, thereby facilitating the degradation of various pollutants.

The results described above demonstrate that SCQDs substantially improve the degradation efficiency of DCF by PM. To gain a deeper understanding of the system's performance, a systematic investigation was undertaken to evaluate the influence of key operational parameters, including solution pH, PM concentration, and SCQDs dosage, on the overall degradation process.

The oxidative capacity of PM varies remarkably under different pH conditions, as previously reported[35]. Initially, the influence of pH on the PM/SCQDs system was explored. As depicted in Fig. 3(a), when the initial pH value (pHini) of the solution is raised from 3 to 9, the removal efficiency of DCF in the PM/SCQDs system drops substantially, from 100 % to a mere 18 %. With the increase in pH, the redox potential of PM gradually diminishes, consequently resulting in a corresponding decrease in its oxidation capacity. Nevertheless, in comparison to the PM alone system, the PM/SCQDs system still demonstrates enhanced pollutant degradation across a broad pH range (i.e., pH = 3, 5, 7, 9). Specifically, the reaction rate constants increase by 2-fold, 13-fold, 2-fold, and 2-fold, respectively (Fig. S5).

Subsequently, the effect of SCQDs dosage on the performance of the PM/SCQDs system was investigated (Fig. 3(b)). The degradation efficiency of pollutants exhibited a trend of initial enhancement followed by inhibition as the SCQDs dosage increased. Specifically, the removal rate of DCF improved from 25 % to 100 % when the SCQDs concentration was increased from 0 mg/L to 6 mg/L. However, a further increase in SCQDs dosage to 12 mg/L resulted in a decline by 49 % in DCF degradation efficiency. This phenomenon can be attributed to the excessive SCQDs concentration potentially interfering with the generation or stability of ROS within the system, thereby compromising its overall oxidative capacity.

As depicted in Fig. 3(c), the degradation of DCF in the PM/SCQDs system is also strongly dependent on the concentration of permanganate. When the permanganate concentration was increased from 30 μM to 50 μM, the degradation rate of DCF improved significantly, rising from 43 % to nearly 100 %. In the absence of permanganate, the removal rate of DCF was negligible, suggesting that the adsorption capacity of SCQDs alone for pollutants is minimal. Furthermore, when the permanganate concentration was further increased from 50 μM to 100 μM, the time required for complete degradation of pollutants was reduced from 20 min to 7 min. This can be attributed to the fact that as the concentration of PM increases, the generation of active species in the system significantly increases. Consequently, the system's ability to degrade pollutants is markedly enhanced. In conclusion, the performance of the PM/SCQDs system is influenced by three key factors: solution pH, SCQDs dosage, and permanganate concentration.

ce:section.ce:para:

2
• −
), and singlet oxygen (1O2) has been proposed as a potential ROS for pollutant degradation, as suggested in previous studies[36]. Radical scavenging experiments were employed to investigate the presence of these potential free radicals in the PM/SCQDs system. Methanol (MA) and tert-butanol (TBA) were used as quenching agents to assess the roles of •OH and
SO
4
• −
. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the addition of 10 mM MA resulted in only slight inhibition of DCF degradation, 10 mM TBA had no observable effect on the system. In Fig. S6, no characteristic EPR signals corresponding to either
O
2
• −
or 1O2 were detected in the PM/SCQDs system. These results demonstrate that reactive oxygen species (•OH,
SO
4
• −
,
O
2
• −
, and 1O2) play a negligible role in the PM/SCQDs system. Additionally, CQDs can generate photogenerated holes (h⁺) under light exposure due to their excellent optical properties[21]. To investigate the potential contribution of h+ in the PM/SCQDs system, a degradation experiment of DCF was performed under dark conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), the absence of light had no impact on the degradation efficiency of DCF, with complete degradation achieved within 20 min. This result indicates that h+ is unlikely to be involved in the degradation process. Overall, the results demonstrate that •OH,
SO
4
• −
, and h+ do not significantly contribute to the degradation of DCF in the PM/SCQDs system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification and role of active manganese species
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have shown that a variety of reactive manganese species (RMnS), including Mn(VI), Mn(V), Mn(IV), and Mn(III), play key roles during enhanced manganate oxidation[37]. Based on these findings, we hypothesized that these RMnS may be present in the PM/SCQDs system and could significantly influence its oxidation reactions. Therefore, we aimed to identify the RMnS within the PM/SCQDs system and elucidate their specific roles.

Mn(VI) and Mn(V): The wavelengths of 610 nm and 660 nm represent the characteristic absorbance peaks corresponding to Mn(VI) and Mn(V), respectively[38]. However, as illustrated in Fig. 4(b), with increasing consumption of PM, no apparent peaks were observed at these wavelengths within 20 min. Furthermore, Gao et al. proposed that high-valent Mn-oxo species can facilitate the oxidation of PMSO to PMSO2 through oxygen atom transfer, resulting in a conversion rate of approximately 100 %. In contrast, low-valent manganese species, such as MnO2 and Mn(III), exhibit negligible degradation of PMSO[39]. Therefore, the existence of Mn(VI) and Mn(V) in the PM/SCQDs process was investigated using PMSO as a probe. As illustrated in Fig. S7 (a)−(b), the addition of SCQDs resulted in the inhibition of both PMSO degradation and PMSO2 generation when compared to the PM system alone. The concentration of PMSO decreased from 20 μM to 10 μM, and the generation of PMSO2 decreased from 20 μM to 10 μM within 20 min. Nevertheless, the conversion rate PMSO2 remained close to 100 % (Fig. S7(c)). The addition of SCQDs may have reduced the formation of Mn(V) and Mn(VI), while promoting the generation of other low-valent manganese species that have no degrading effect on PMSO. In summary, the above findings indicate that the contribution of Mn(VI) and Mn(V) in the PM/SCQDs process is negligible.

MnO
2
: Given that the reduction of Mn(VII) is frequently accompanied by the in-situ formation of MnO2, which can accelerate the oxidation process[40–42], the potential role of MnO2 in the PM/SCQDs system was investigated. The UV–vis spectra of the PM/SCQDs system in the absence of contaminants were acquired (Fig. 4(b)). At pH = 5.0, the characteristic peak of permanganate at 525 nm rapidly weakened, while the absorbance in the range of 300–400 nm showed a sharp increase within the first minute, followed by a slower growth until reaching a maximum at 20 min. Based on the absorbance of PM and MnO2 in the UV–vis spectra, the amounts of PM consumed and MnO2 produced were determined according to the method described in Text S4.

With the addition of SCQDs, the PM concentration decreased from an initial 50 μM to 15 μM within 1 min, accompanied by the generation of 27 μM MnO2. After 20 min, only 5 μM PM remained, while 39 μM of MnO2 was generated (Fig. 4(c)). These results indicate that the consumption of PM in the PM/SCQDs system occurs rapidly, coinciding with substantial production of MnO2.

Previous studies have shown that MnO2 exhibits adsorption, oxidation, and catalytic properties[40,41]. To evaluate the adsorptive capacity of MnO2 in the PM/SCQDs system, hydroxylamine was employed as a terminator, and the samples were not subjected to membrane filtration. The adsorbed pollutants were released by reducing MnO2 to Mn2+ using hydroxylamine. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the adsorption experiment revealed that the degradation rate of DCFremained unaffected, achieving 100 % complete degradation within 20 min. This finding effectively rules out the possibility of adsorption by the in-situ formed MnO2 in the PM/SCQDs system.

The oxidative properties of MnO2 were further investigated by adding ex-situ formed MnO2 (designated as MnO2-0) to the system. To explore the potential influence of SCQDs on MnO2 oxidation, SCQDs were added during the preparation of MnO2 (designated as MnO2-1). The detailed preparation procedure for ex-situ MnO2 is provided in Text S3. At pH = 5, MnO2-0 removed 59 % of DCF within 20 min, confirming its oxidative capability. Notably, the oxidation performance of MnO2-1 toward DCF was consistent with that of MnO2-0 (Fig. 5(b)), indicating that the addition of SCQDs does not influence the oxidative performance of MnO2. Furthermore, XPS analysis of the PM/SCQDs reaction products (Fig. S8) revealed that the primary products were MnO2, with Mn(IV) and Mn(III) contents of 61.29 % and 38.71 %, respectively. As reported by Luo et al., the content of Mn(III) in MnO2 significantly enhances its oxidative capacity[43].

The catalytic properties of MnO2 were evaluated by preparing ex-situ MnO2 and conducting degradation experiments with DCF using PM, MnO2, and PM/MnO2 systems. As shown in Fig. S9, the degradation rate of DCF in the PM/MnO2 system was nearly equivalent to the sum of the degradation rates achieved by PM and MnO2 individually. This suggests that MnO2 does not act as a catalyst in the PM/SCQDs process but rather functions primarily as an oxidizing agent. Therefore, the oxidative properties of the generated MnO2 play a key role in enhancing the oxidation efficiency of PM in the presence of SCQDs.

Mn(Ⅲ): Mn(III) can form complexes with pyrophosphate (PP), which is commonly used to investigate the multifaceted role of Mn(Ⅲ) in various processes[44,45]. To explore the role of Mn(III) in the PM/SCQDs system, we employed a UV-Vis spectrophotometer to monitor the absorbance at 258 nm, which corresponds to the characteristic absorption peak of the Mn(III)-PP complex. The experiments were conducted at pH 5.0, and the results are depicted in Fig. 5(c). In the presence of PP without pollutants, the characteristic absorption peak of PM at 525 nm exhibited a rapid decline within 1 min, while the absorbance of Mn(Ⅲ)-PP increased. As the reaction proceeded, the absorbance of Mn(III)-PP continued to increase and its growth rate gradually slowed down, indicating the rapid generation and stable existence of Mn(III)-PP in the PM/SCQDs system. Furthermore, the addition of PP to the PM/SCQDs system adversely impacted the degradation of DCF, reducing the degradation rate from 100 % to 57 % (Fig. 5(d)). This inhibition can be attributed to the formation of the Mn(III)-PP complex, which diminishes the oxidative capacity of Mn(III) and consequently hinders the degradation of DCF. These experimental results demonstrate that the PM/SCQDs system effectively promotes the generation of Mn(III), which plays a significant role in the oxidative degradation of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of SCQDs in the PM/SCQDs process
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, SCQDs were found to enhance the degradation efficiency of DCF by PM. However, the specific mechanisms by which SCQDs facilitate this process remain unclear and require further investigation. Previous studies have demonstrated that electron-rich sites on the surfaces of biochar and CNTs can promote the decomposition of Mn(VII), leading to the formation of highly reactive intermediate manganese species, which substantially improve the degradation efficiency of organic pollutant[12,16]. To explore the role of electron-rich sites in SCQDs, we compared the effects of pristine SCQDs and ozone pre-oxidized SCQDs on DCF degradation by PM, as illustrated in Fig. 6(a). Clearly, ozone pre-oxidation significantly weakened the catalytic effect of SCQDs, reducing the DCF removal rate from 100 % to 69 %. This result clearly demonstrates that the electron-rich sites on SCQDs are critical for enhancing DCF degradation by PM.

Furthermore, we compared the FTIR spectra of CQDs and SCQDs, identifying distinct peaks corresponding to CS, C–S/C–S–C, and –SOₓ groups in SCQDs (Fig. 6(b)). These findings suggest that these sulfur-containing functional groups play a pivotal role in accelerating permanganate consumption and generating reactive manganese species. To gain deeper insights, XPS analysis was conducted on the SCQDs after reaction with permanganate (Fig. S10). The fine structures of C 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p spectra indicated a weakening of C–S bonds and the disappearance of the –C–SOₓ peak after the reaction. These observations confirm that CS, C–S, and –C–SOₓ groups in SCQDs are essential for enhancing the permanganate oxidation process. The incorporation of sulfur atoms into the CQD introduces additional electronic state. Due to the relatively high electronegativity of sulfur compared to carbon, these dopant atoms act as electron donors, facilitating efficient electron transfer to the CQD surface and thereby increasing its electron density. As supported by previous studies, the introduction of sulfur species not only modulates the spin density and charge distribution but also generates additional active sites, thereby enhancing the material's reactivity[46]. Based on these results, the CS, C-S, and -C-SOx groups on the SCQDs surface serve as electron-rich sites. These sites promote the transformation of PM into highly intermediate reactive species, significantly boosting the system’s capacity for the oxidative degradation of pollutants.

In summary, this study proposes a mechanism diagram illustrating how SCQDs enhance the PM system (see Scheme 1). When permanganate is used alone to oxidize DCF, the reaction proceeds at a relatively slow rate. With the introduction of SCQDs, DCF is completely degraded. This enhancement is attributed to the CS, C–S, and -SOₓ functional groups on the surface of SCQDs, which facilitate the decomposition of permanganate. This electron-rich site promotes the formation of more reactive Mn(III) and Mn(IV) species, thereby significantly improving the oxidative degradation of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidation performance in complicated water matrix
ce:section.ce:para: The PM/SCQDs system exhibits robust resistance to interference from cations, anions, and natural organic matter (NOM), which are commonly present in aqueous environments. As shown in Fig. 7(a)–(f), the degradation efficiency of DCF in the PM/SCQDs system exhibited only a slight inhibition, decreasing from 100 % to 75 %, upon the addition of 1 mM Ca2+ or 1 mM Mg2+, respectively. However, as the concentrations of Ca2+ and Mg2+ were increased from 1 mM to 5 mM, the degradation efficiency of DCF remained relatively stable. Previous studies have shown that the presence of cations triggers the aggregation of MnO2 [47]. As a result, the aggregation of MnO2 reduces the active sites on the surface of manganese dioxide thereby reducing its oxidizing activity. To further evaluate the impact of NOM on the PM/SCQDs system, humic acid (HA) was used as a representative NOM. The results indicated that the addition of HA at various concentrations enhanced the degradation of DCF to different extents. Specifically, the addition of 5 mg/L HA significantly reduced the DCF degradation time from 20 min to 5 min. According to Zhou et al., HA not only promotes the generation of active manganese species but also facilitates electron transfer from DCF to permanganate, thereby significantly improving the removal efficiency of DCF[48]. Additionally, the influence of common anions on the PM/SCQDs system was investigated. Experimental results revealed that the removal efficiency of DCF remained largely unaffected even when the concentrations of anions such as Cl-, HCO3 -, and NO3 - were elevated to 5 mM. These results demonstrate that the PM/SCQDs system exhibits superior performance in complex water matrices, which is advantageous for its further practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathway and toxicity evolution of DCF
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, UPLC- IMS QTOF-MS was used to analyze the intermediate products generated during the degradation of DCF in the PM/SCQDs system. As shown in Fig. S11, nine intermediate products were identified. Based on the analysis of these intermediates, we have proposed four potential degradation pathways for DCF by PM/SCQDs, denoted as Pathways I, II, III, and IV ( Fig. 8(a)). In pathway I, the C−N bond undergoes cleavage to generate the intermediate product TP1.In pathway II, at the adjacent position of the nitrogen atom in the benzene ethanolic ring, DCF undergoes a hydroxylation reaction to produce TP2, the hydroxyl group of TP2 is further oxidized to generate TP3, and then the carboxyl group in TP3 is dehydrated to form TP4. In Pathway II, P5 is formed by the decarboxylation reaction of DCF and then oxidized to P6. Subsequently, it undergoes dechlorination to form TP7. In the degradation path Ⅳ, DCF decarboxylates to form TP8, followed by dechlorination to form TP9. In summary, the degradation of DCF encompasses hydrogenation, decarboxylation, dehydrogenation, dechlorination, formylation, and C−N cleavage procedures.

To further understand the toxicity of the intermediates produced by DCF treated with PM/SCQDs, we calculated the toxicity of these intermediates using a toxicity estimation software tool (T.E.S.T., U.S. EPA). As illustrated in Fig. 8(b), after being treated by the PM/SCQDs system, the oral rat LD50 of these DCF intermediate products was increased, suggesting a reduction in toxicity. Additionally, the mutagenicity and developmental toxicity of most of these degradation products were decreased in Fig. 8(c) and Fig. 8(d). In conclusion, the toxicity of DCF was reduced after PM/SCQDs treatment, mitigating its environmental impact.

This study demonstrates that the addition of SCQDs can significantly enhance the ability of permanganate for water contaminant degradation, outperforming both PM alone and the PM/CQDs system. Experimental results and characterization reveal that the sulfur-containing functional groups on SCQDs play a crucial role in facilitating the oxidation process of Mn(VII). Additionally, the rapid formation of Mn(III) and Mn(IV) species in the PM/SCQDs system further contributes to the effective oxidation and removal of contaminants. This finding highlights a novel approach to enhancing permanganate-based oxidation through the use of carbon-based materials.

Moreover, the PM/SCQDs system exhibits remarkable anti-interference capabilities, effectively mitigating the inhibitory effects of common water matrix components, including cations, anions, and natural organic matter (NOM). Notably, the system demonstrates significant detoxification efficacy, substantially reducing the ecotoxicity of DCF and its potential adverse impacts on aquatic ecosystems. These findings collectively highlight the practical applicability and environmental advantages of the PM/SCQDs system as an efficient and sustainable solution for water purification and contaminant remediation.

In this study, SCQDs demonstrated remarkable efficacy in enhancing the degradation of DCF and various pollutants by permanganate. As an emerging carbon-based material, SCQDs offer a novel and promising approach in the field of water treatment. The PM/SCQDs system exhibited robust anti-interference capabilities while simultaneously minimizing the formation of toxic by-products, highlighting its environmentally beneficial properties. These findings provide a solid foundation for the practical application of this technology in real water systems, with significant potential for widespread use in the remediation of contaminated water.
